item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, but I agree that the final note about the electromagnetic cloud adds nothing to the story, nor does the reemergence. I think giving a bit more detail about how the phenomenon came to an end and classifying it as Neutralized would give it more a sense of completeness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An entertaining read, but I'm inclined to question the "safe" designation given the nature of the SCP's re-emergence and unpredictable nature (not to mention, my reading of it gives me the impression that it's relatively "uncontained" to a degree at this time). Still, nicely written!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I used to longboard in Safety Harbor, it's also where I proposed to my fiance. I will probably think of this SCP the next time I'm back there.

item.title: Questions for the author
item.content:encoded: Just wondering, by any chance "InsipidParoxysm" do you happen to come from Pinellas?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt like the very last collapsible just killed it. Too much explaining for me, and I don't really like the idea that the Foundation can just wave some instruments at a haunting and say "yep, we have measurable ghost activity, we know exactly what this is now."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not only not clear on what is going on here, but on what things actually happened and in what order.

First, a few nitpicks
- I wouldn't say it if I didn't care

If the milk bottles refilled themselves when placed on the porch, aren't the bottles themselves anomalous and should have been taken? The last addendum makes it sound like they did but I didn't see that in the old containment procedures.

However, the Second Life Labor Service is also to be implemented

What the heck is this? It is never brought up again.

The official cover story during containment is to establish that the area is currently undergoing sensitive relations with Native American peoples. Similarly, those who attempt to leave Safety Harbor are to be deterred with the news that nearby areas are undergoing conflict with Native American peoples.

That hardly feels like a reason to keep people from entering or leaving town. "I'm sorry sir, you can't pass this checkpoint. It might upset the Seminole tribe." I like the feel of the town adapting, but it might make more sense in a remote area. How long can they keep people from entering or leaving town? While we are on the subject, how long did the re-animations keep going for? You made it sound like the milk bottle exchange ended in 1939.

The meeting transcript implies that this was the first encounter with the anomaly related to SCP-2488.

Does this mean it is just their first contact with the re-animations? Unless somebody drank the mystery milk for breakfast and dropped dead into his eggs before the next meeting, they would all have been familiar with the magic milk bottles.

DOCUMENT 2488 17-C doesn't seem to contribute anything. You introduced the church in the document above it as being pro milk-zombie. Now you are saying there is dissent but it doesn't go anywhere.

This recovered documentation was discovered as investigation into SCP-2488 was initiated.
It was, as the mayor said, a storage space full of heritage documents that they didn't dare to go through, lest they damage or lose any of them.
The mayor couldn't tell us much, other than that it was some old folklore rolling around the town.

These notes make it look like the Foundation explored the phenomenon through the documents they found but earlier it sounded like they found it right after the newspaper clippings about the re-animations.

This is followed up by the last record which is clearly a modern view looking into an event that is over.

The anomaly only took a few hours to discover.

What does this mean? The new milk bottles? The radiation?

+ Maybe I am thinking about his too much
- I have had a few days to stew over it.

There is a lot of this that I can't seem to make any sense out of. The first and probably dumbest one is: why milk? Before the importation of cattle, horses, and goats Native Americans did not have any milk-producing livestock and like other populations with no dairy to eat they would probably have been largely lactose intolerant.

It is possible that a milk bottle is one of the pieces of litter that was left behind on the mound and maybe that had something to do with it.

Finally, I don't understand the manual labor bit. A re-animated corpse is good for 3 to 5 weeks. The average human lives about seventy years and works for about 50. There can't be more than one active corpse for every 750 able bodied people. How helpful can that be?


Things I did like: the eulogy, the fact the someone else covered up the newspaper story, the church helping to release the corpses, and the milk bottle in the mound. There is a strong sense of history here, but I have to downvote over the confused timeline and self-contradictions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, most people used the term Indians back then (a lot of official documents even used that name) but some people used the scholarly/more formal "native tribes" or "indigenous peoples" of America.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
CONTAINMENT PROCEDURES, 1933 TO 1939

Does that mean that they devised these procedures in 1933, and if so, did they use the term "native american" back then? I think back then they just called them Indians. Or natives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad to see this up on the site, I know you put a lot of work into it, and it shows. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems to be some language problems? For example "The researchers managed to translate the conglomerated signals into a more translatable product." seems awkward to read. They translated them into something that could was more able to be translated than the thing that had been translated? Even if there is a way that it makes sense that I'm not seeing - And that's not sarcasm, by the way, I am super tired - It still just feels awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay - zombies and random magic items have been done before, better and worse, but the spin you put on it and the writing was done well enough to eke an upvote for me.

This definitely would work better with or as a tale, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Our hypothesis is that their consciousnesses are all still here. It correlates, somewhat, to the native belief of ancestral spirits. We don't know how many there are, we're still checking the census and burial records, but it doesn't seem like it will be a small number.

Oh shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was trying to imply the whole "not sure" by saying that it was "known" to do so, rather than solidly say that it gave this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When consumed, SCP-2488 was known to give the consumer increased physical capability.

Yet the third footnote stated that there were no increased capabilities. Is this intentional?
